title: People & Family
summary: Words for the people around you.

teach.note: Used when addressing or referring to your father.
Specifically your mother's mother in traditional usage.
Also an affectionate word for anything small.

grammar.title: Family words are always 'someone's'
grammar.body:
In Cherokee you can't just say 'mother' in the abstract — kinship terms always belong to someone. The forms here (**ᎡᏥ** etsi, **ᎡᏙᏓ** edoda) are the everyday forms you use for *your own* family.
Cherokee families are traditionally **matrilineal**: clan identity passes through the mother, which is why terms like ᎡᎵᏏ (elisi, maternal grandmother) are so central.

exercises.prompt: Which family member did you hear?
Cherokee clan identity traditionally passes through…

teach.note: Notice: ᎠᎨᏯ (woman) + the ending from ᎠᏧᏣ (boy).

culture.title: Aniyvwiya — the Principal People
culture.body: The word 'Cherokee' comes from other languages. In their own language, Cherokee people call themselves **ᎠᏂᏴᏫᏯ** (Aniyvwiya), 'the Principal People' or 'Real People', and the language **ᏣᎳᎩ** (Tsalagi). Today Cherokee is spoken in three federally recognized tribes: the Cherokee Nation and the United Keetoowah Band in Oklahoma, and the Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians in North Carolina.
